description: A group of thousands of man-made chemicals used in many industries and consumer products. Known as 'forever chemicals' due to persistence in the environment.
A policy approach where producers are given significant responsibility for the treatment or disposal of post-consumer products.

full: Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals
description: EU chemical regulation that manages the risks posed by chemicals. Requires companies to register substances and provide safety data.
US federal law regulating the introduction of new or already existing chemicals. Administered by the EPA.
Substances identified under REACH as having serious and often irreversible effects on human health or the environment. Placed on the REACH Candidate List.
A synthetic perfluorocarbon that is a PFAS compound. Used in manufacturing of fluoropolymers. Now largely phased out in the US.
A PFAS compound formerly used in Scotchgard and firefighting foam. Listed as a persistent organic pollutant under the Stockholm Convention.
A synthetic fluoropolymer used extensively as a coating (e.g., Teflon). A PFAS-adjacent compound under scrutiny in some regulatory frameworks.
The highest level of a contaminant that is allowed in drinking water. Set by the EPA under the Safe Drinking Water Act.
A non-enforceable public health goal for contaminants in drinking water. Often zero for carcinogens.
The primary federal law ensuring safe drinking water. EPA sets standards for drinking water quality.

full: Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act
description: Also known as Superfund. Governs cleanup of contaminated sites.
US federal law governing the disposal of solid and hazardous waste.

full: Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act
description: US federal statute that governs the distribution, sale, and use of pesticides.
US federal law regulating air emissions from stationary and mobile sources.
The primary federal law governing water pollution in the US.
US federal agency responsible for environmental protection and enforcement of environmental laws.
EU agency that manages the technical, scientific, and administrative aspects of REACH and other EU chemical legislation.

full: Office of Environmental Health Hazard Assessment
description: California agency responsible for the scientific and policy functions of the California Environmental Protection Agency, including administering Prop 65.
California state department responsible for regulating hazardous waste and toxic substances.
State-level environmental agency (Oregon, Michigan, Idaho, Montana). Enforces state environmental regulations.
Minnesota state agency responsible for environmental protection, including PFAS water standards.
Same as EPA — U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.
US federal agency ensuring safe and healthful working conditions.
US independent agency protecting the public from unreasonable risks of injury or death from consumer products.
US federal agency responsible for protecting public health through food, drug, and device safety.
US agency that enforces trade laws including the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) through import detention orders.
US federal agency overseeing securities markets, including Conflict Minerals reporting (Dodd-Frank Section 1502).
A bill introduced in the state or US Senate. E.g., CA SB 54 (plastic packaging).
A bill introduced in the state Assembly (lower chamber).
A bill introduced in the state or US House of Representatives.
A bill introduced in certain state Senates (e.g., Minnesota SF).
A bill introduced in certain state Houses (e.g., Minnesota HF).
US law creating a rebuttable presumption that goods made in Xinjiang involve forced labor, prohibiting their import.
EU directive requiring large companies to conduct due diligence on human rights and environmental impacts in their value chains.
EU directive requiring large companies to report on sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities.
Reporting standards under CSRD specifying how companies should disclose sustainability information.
EU regulation prohibiting companies from placing certain commodities/products associated with deforestation on the EU market.
EU directive restricting hazardous substances in electrical and electronic equipment.
EU directive setting collection, recycling, and recovery targets for electrical goods.
Chemicals that persist in the environment, accumulate in living organisms, and pose risks to human health. Regulated under the Stockholm Convention.

full: California Safe Drinking Water and Toxic Enforcement Act of 1986
description: California law requiring businesses to provide warnings before knowingly exposing anyone to listed chemicals. Enforced by 60-day notice actions.
EPA program that evaluates substitutes for ozone-depleting substances under the Clean Air Act.
An early-stage regulatory notice inviting public comment before a formal proposed rule is published.
A formal regulatory document published in the Federal Register inviting public comment on a proposed rule.
Announcement of grant or funding opportunities from a federal or state agency.
The official journal of the federal government of the United States, containing government agency rules, proposed rules, and public notices.
The codification of rules published by the executive departments and agencies of the federal government.
The codification of general and permanent laws of the United States.
EU strategy for a mix of chemicals regulations to achieve a toxic-free environment.

full: Substances of Concern In articles as such or in complex objects (Products)
description: ECHA database for articles containing SVHC in concentration above 0.1% w/w.
Document listing hazard information, handling, storage, and emergency procedures for a substance. Required under GHS/REACH.

full: Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals
description: International system for standardizing chemical hazard communication.
The four minerals covered by Dodd-Frank Section 1502 (Conflict Minerals Rule). Required to be reported on SEC Form SD.
Standardized industry template for reporting conflict minerals due diligence, maintained by the Responsible Minerals Initiative (RMI).
Organization providing tools and resources to companies for conducting responsible mineral sourcing due diligence.
Audit program operated by RMI to assess smelter/refiner compliance with responsible sourcing standards.
Central African country; conflict minerals legislation focuses on minerals originating from DRC and adjoining countries.

full: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
description: International organization providing guidance on responsible business conduct including the Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals.
A non-profit or industry body established to implement EPR programs on behalf of producers.
Material recycled from products after use by consumers. Many EPR laws set minimum PCR content requirements.
Manufacturing waste recycled back into production. Distinct from PCR for regulatory purposes.
Plastic items intended to be used only once. Subject to bans and restrictions in many jurisdictions.

full: Free from Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances
description: Descriptor used in packaging regulations (e.g., food contact materials) prohibiting intentional addition of PFAS.
Non-profit organization overseeing development of voluntary consensus standards for products, services, processes, systems, and personnel in the US.

full: American Society for Testing and Materials
description: International standards organization developing and publishing voluntary consensus technical standards.
Independent international organization that develops and publishes international standards.
International standards organization preparing and publishing international standards for electrical and electronic technologies.
Discipline concerned with protecting workers, the public, and the environment. Common abbreviation in manufacturing and compliance contexts.
Framework for assessing a company's business practices and performance on sustainability and ethical issues.
Gases that trap heat in the atmosphere. Includes CO2, methane, nitrous oxide, and fluorinated gases.
Organic chemicals with high vapor pressure at room temperature. Regulated under the Clean Air Act for air quality impacts.
Pollutants known or suspected to cause cancer or other serious health effects. Listed under the Clean Air Act.
EPA standards for outdoor air quality for six principal pollutants (criteria pollutants).
EPA program requiring facilities to develop plans to prevent oil spills into navigable waters.
EPA program tracking release and management of toxic chemicals by US facilities. Requires annual Form R reporting.
Standard used by federal statistical agencies to classify business establishments.

full: Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act
description: Federal law requiring facilities to report toxic chemical releases and emergency planning information to EPA and state agencies; relevant for windows/doors manufacturers using or releasing PFAS chemicals.
Federal statute establishing source reduction and pollution prevention programs; works alongside EPCRA to mandate reporting of certain chemicals including newly-listed PFAS under TRI.
Annual fiscal period used by EPA for regulatory implementation timelines; referenced for mandatory PFAS reporting year compliance deadlines.

full: Product Registry and Inventory System for Minnesota
description: Minnesota's chemical reporting system requiring manufacturers to register products containing PFAS under Amara's Law; critical compliance requirement for companies selling products in Minnesota.
A reporting metric used in Minnesota's PRISM system for PFAS product reporting, requiring manufacturers to quantify and report commercially distributed units containing PFAS.
The New Mexico state environmental regulatory agency tasked with implementing and reviewing the state's PFAS Protection Act, including exemptions for fluoropolymer-based products.
Federal legislation that included mandates for EPA action on PFAS, including requirements to add perfluoroalkyl substances to the Toxics Release Inventory.
The ECHA committee responsible for evaluating and providing opinions on proposed chemical restrictions under EU REACH, including the universal PFAS restriction currently under consideration.
Australia's regulatory scheme for imported and manufactured industrial chemicals that is requiring information submissions on 522 PFAS substances, potentially preceding future use restrictions.
Threshold concept in EPA's proposed narrowed TSCA Section 8(a)(7) PFAS reporting rule that may exempt very small quantities of PFAS from reporting obligations.
